sentences.text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]
I was born November 13, '09, in Dziedzice.
It's a small town near Bielsko-Biala.
When I was born, this part of the country belonged to Austria.
When the war broke out, first of all, I was born-- I was seven months a baby.
The doctor gave up.
He said I would not live.
So my grandmother bundled me up and took me to her hometown.
And I was a raised for almost four years by my grandparents.
I didn't know my parents.
They came from time to time to visit us.
But there were other sisters and brothers who came.
So I did not.
In 1915, my parents moved from Austria to Germany.
It was close, all in the same neighborhood on the border.
And they picked me up.
I refused to go.
I said this to my parents.
I don't want to go with you.
But as a child, you get used to it.
I had an older brother-- one year older.
And we moved to Tarnowiec.
In Tarnowiec, I was a grown up.
I was raised there.
I attended elementary school for four years and then eight years high school.
This was a German high school.
The mother language was German.
And in 1922, after World War I ended in 1918, Poland was established.
And they got a lot of parts of Austria, of Russia, and the part of Germany too.
In 1922, we became Polish, our part of the country, our city.
Most of the German Jews, they ran away.
They went to Germany.
And a few remained, and my family remained too.
The high school was taken away by the Poles.
And we, the minority people, the Jews, the German Catholics and Protestants, we built a new school.
Again, the mother language was German.
But in the second grade of high school, we had to study Polish.
In the third grade, we studied with French.
In the fourth grade, English.
And only in the first grade, we studied with Latin.
I had eight years Latin.
I graduated in 1928.
And during the whole time of my school years, we were two Jews only.
But we never felt any anti-Semitism in school-- not at all.
And after '28, after graduation, I went to Germany.
They have over the border about a half an hour by train to a pharmacy is an apprentice in pharmacy for two years.
My friend, he went to Germany to a university to study at the university-- I think law.
He studied law.
After the two years, I entered the army, the Polish Army.
Since I was a graduate of the high school, I had the right to join the officer school.
The Polish didn't like Jewish officers.
And they, instead of taking them in, they said, oh, he is sick or something, and they released him.
They would rather release him instead of keeping him in the officer school.
But with me, it was different.
It was in a small town.
Everybody knew me.
I was a very well known athlete in soccer and in tennis.
So they couldn't refuse it.
So they were supposed to take me to the sanitary, to the medical corps, but I don't know if it was on purpose or by mistake, this short name for the medical corps was SAT, Sanitary.
When they brought SAP, SAP are the sappers.
And I had to serve for two years with the pioneers building bridges and pontoons.
And we learned how to mine bridges and highways.
And I couldn't get out.
I tried a few times to change.
But it was impossible.
I had to stay there.
And after 18 months in the officer school, I was attached in Krakow to the regiment to our formation-- to the sappers.
About one and a half year being already in the army, one Sunday in the evening, we were already sleeping.
A soldier from the gate comes to our barrack and calls my name.
I said, what do you want?
You have to get dressed and come to the gate.
I said, why?
You get a pass to go to the city.
I said, I didn't ask for it.
It's an order.
I had to get dressed.
I come to the gate.
And the officer tells me, here is your pass.
And somebody in front is waiting for you.
I go to the gate and an older man.
But I recognize right away he was not dressed like a European, like an American-- like clothes, fancy.
And he said, I'm your uncle.
Now, this was in 1932.
They came to visit us from the United States.
And I took him out to the city.
We went to eat.
And we went to a Yiddish theatre with him.
And we talked about what I'm doing and how long.
And I explained to him I'm supposed to serve in the medical corps.
But they put me here that I had to serve.
And this was fine.
I said goodbye at night.
I returned to the barracks.
And after about, I would say, four or five weeks, I was called to the general of the division in Krakow.
My God, what did I do?
I didn't do nothing.
I didn't commit any crimes.
And he tells me, from tomorrow on, you go by streetcar every morning to the hospital, to the pharmacy, and there you will be doing your duty.
For last six months, I didn't do any exercises.
And I went in the morning dressed up to the pharmacy.
They couldn't transfer me permanently there.
But I had to go by streetcar.
This was up to the end.
This was the last six months.
So I got away with it.
After serving in the army the two years, I went to the university in Krakow to pharmacy school.
And I was there for three years.
I graduated in '35.
But I returned not to my hometown Tarnowiec, but to the capital of the state, Katowice.
And there, I started to work as a pharmacist in two pharmacies.
During the time, I was naturally very active in Zionist youth organizations from childhood on.
And I was five years old.
The first I belonged was on the German time yet.
I belonged to the Blau-Weiss.
It was a German Jewish youth organization.
And all we did-- they taught the songs and a little history.
On Sunday, we took trips or hikes.
And already during the trips and hikes, I was always concerned about my next ones.
When we went on a bicycle ride, I saw a girl or a boy stood behind could make it so fast, I went back and I helped them.
I was always worried about other ones.
I tried to help.
And this was my nature.
And later on, when I returned from the university in Katowice, I met a friend from before, a longtime friend.
And we talked.
He said, what are you doing?
I said, I'm a pharmacist.
But I  mean in your free time?
I said, nothing.
He said, why don't you come to the Akiba.
This is the general Zionist youth organization on the more religious side.
And you might enjoy it.
And I went to see him in the evening.
I met other people there-- the members and the board of directors.
And I went another night and another night.
And I started to join it.
And in no time after a couple of weeks, they put me already on the board of directors.
And in a few months, I became the leader of the organization in Katowice.
And we had branches naturally all over Poland.
And in our state of Silesia, there were branches too.
And it became the leader of the state.
The activities differed.
We had meetings about history.
And we had like, for instance, I became in charge of the Keren Kayemet, of the national fund-- Israel National Fund.
And I was elected to the Zionist organization as a delegate to the congress.
And those were my activities.
In the summer, we arranged summer camps.
First, I arranged the summer camp for our state only.
And we went in the mountains.
And I was in charge of the camp-- about 400, 500 young boys and girls.
And we spent four weeks in the mountains in the camp.
After this camp in July, in August, we had the camp for all Poland, for all members in Poland in the mountains, in the Tatra Mountains.
This was on the Polish-Czechoslovakian border high up in the mountains.
This is part of the Carpathian.
And there, there were mostly leaders.
And we spent there four weeks too.
In '39, it was about the end of the camp.
In the last few days, we formed groups.
And we went for hikes in the mountains.
And I was supposed to be one of the leaders of the groups.
But I told them, listen, I cannot go.
I have to go back to work.
My vacation is through.
And I have to be back.
And I went back.
And somebody else took over.
And when I returned home, in the morning, I got up in order to go to the pharmacy.
I turned on the radio when I heard a terrible accident in the mountains.
They ran in bad weather in thunder and lightning.
And the leader of the group which I was supposed to lead got killed.
And many were hurt.
I didn't go back to work.
I went to the next train back to the mountains.
And I helped in the rescues naturally.
But even in the camps, I was always in care of the ambulance.
I was a doctor in the camps.
There were sick people.
And then if somebody was really serious sick, I took him down to the city.
And the doctor took over.
And this was the end.
In that time, I was engaged to a girl.
And we decided naturally to go to Palestine.
I sent her in '39 in April to Palestine in order that I would be soon following.
But I couldn't do it because I was in charge of the illegal Aliyah, of the illegal immigration from Poland to enter Palestine.
And my friends said, Sig, you have time, you can always go because my papers were ready.
But I stayed another month and other months.
And it came September 1, when the war broke out.
And we were right on the border.
The Germans entered in the morning, September 1, crossed the border without declaring war and started to enter Poland.
But the night before, they bombarded all Polish airports.
And for this reason, not one Polish airplane could go up and fight the Germans, all gone.
Right in the morning, when I  saw the Germans ordered in, I went to the station and took the last train to Krakow.
This was south of Katowice from Silesia.
I joined my army.
And we retreated, my formation, the battalion.
All we did-- sometimes we mined a road or a highway.
This was all we did.
We couldn't resist the powerful German Army.
We retreated.
For about 10 days, we marched and marched eastwards.
And one morning, we got up, and we faced two fronts.
In front of us, the Russians, who didn't declare war.
They were not fighting the Poles.
And in the back, naturally, the Germans.
We didn't know what to do.
Our colonel said to us, stay here.
I'm going for new orders.
And we will see what we have to do.
And we were waiting for him.
And I'm waiting up today for him.
He never showed up.
Maybe he got killed.
Maybe he was caught or he ran away.
We don't know.
No sign about it.
So we younger officers-- we decided to dissolve the formation.
We told the soldiers, go where you want to.
Now, we officers, all of my colleagues, decided naturally they are going to the Russians.
Why should they go to the Germans?
The Germans are our enemies.
I was the only one who decided, no.
I'm going back home because I want to see what my family is doing-- my parents, my brother, and my sister.
And naturally, I got rid of my uniform, my identifications.
I bought this suit.
And I started to march westwards back to the Germans.
The first five minutes I marched, maybe five, six minutes, is now a German soldier-- in German, what are you doing here?
And since I speak those languages, I told him in German I was forcefully evacuated by the Poles.
So he took me for a German.
He never asked me if I was  a Jew, because at that time at the beginning, there was no Jewish questions.
They didn't know-- there was no orders what to do with the Jews.
And he said, oh, he welcomed me.
And now go back to your hometown.
It's already in Germany.
And they helped me even with transportation and trucks and cars-- either way.
And in a few days, I was already home.
We marched 10 days.
But maybe two,  three days I was home already.
The other colleagues who went to Russia-- there is not one alive.
They all were killed in '40 in Katyn by the Russians because the Russians not only killed the military officers, but the whole Polish intelligentsia-- professors and teachers and doctors and lawyers.
They want to get rid of the Polish culture.
They killed them right away.
So I was the only one alive.
And you went back home.
I went back home.
I came home.
Naturally the temple was already destroyed.
And most of the Jews fled.
The German Jews to Germany.
And the Polish Jews, who in the meantime, came to Katowice, to Silesia-- they went back to Poland.
And I did not know what to do.
About two, three days being at home at Katowice, my father tells me, you know, your former boss in the pharmacy in Tarnowiec, he's looking for you, because he doesn't speak German, only Polish.
And he cannot communicate with the German military.
He had to deliver to the German hospital medications.
And he couldn't communicate with them.
So he said, why don't you go to Auschwitz?
He has a pharmacy there.
And you can work there.
I can help him.
I went to Auschwitz.
And he embraced me and was so happy I came.
I said, what do you want me to do?
I want you to go home every day in the evening, take care of medications, bring medications from Katowice to Auschwitz.
You mean the City of Auschwitz.
To the City of Auschwitz, yeah.
But I said, it's a border.
I cannot cross the border between Silesia and the Galicia at Auschwitz was a border.
And he said, you go to the commandant in Auschwitz.
And you tell him what you want.
I go up to office.
And adjutant, a high officer comes, what do I want?
I said, I want to talk to the commandant.
And what is your name?
I gave him my name.
And I waited a minute, a couple minutes.
He opens the door and leads me to an office, a huge office.
This was in the hospital-- a huge office.
In the back behind the desk sits an older officer.
I recognize right away on the epaulets he's a doctor, because they have the snake, the doctors.
And he said, sit down.
I was wondering, me?
I'm a Jew.
He tells me to sit down.
I sit down.
What is your name?
I gave him my name.
Is your father alive?
I said, yes.
What is his name?
Leopold.
And he smiles.
He said you know, I know your father very well.
Your father came in 1915 to Breslau, to Wroclaw, now Poland, to be checked if he is well enough to go to the army, to the Austrian Army, because we were Austrians, you see?
And he said, I remember him.
From '15 to '39, he remembered the name of my father.
What do you want?
I explained to him.
He gave me a permit that I am working for the pharmacy which supplies the medications to the hospital, to the German hospital.
And I have to go every day by train by Silesia to Auschwitz.
Fine.
I traveled about maybe a week.
One night I'm in the train.
Two SS men come in.
Are Jews here?
I said, yes, I am a Jew.
And I show him the permit.
They looked over, tear it apart, out.
They threw me out of the train.
I went to Auschwitz, back to the city, stayed overnight.
I went to the pharmacy.
I told him what happened.
He said, go back to the commandant.
I went to him.
He was furious.
Who do those people think they are?
I am the commandant here.
And they tore apart my permit.
Well, he gave me a much stronger one.
Fine, an evening, I go back, again, the same thing.
They are coming in and throwing me out.
But I didn't go anymore to Auschwitz.
I walked at night from Auschwitz from the station to the next station which belonged already to Silesia.
There I went to the train, and I was already home.
Then I had been back to Auschwitz anymore.
While I was in Auschwitz, one day, I found out that the parents of my fiance were in Auschwitz, older people, very religious people.
And the sister too-- the sister was living already in Israel, in Palestine.
But every year, she and her husband came as delegates from Israel to the summer camps, and here they got stuck.
And she is there too.
What shall she do?
I said, you know, the parents I cannot help.
But you come with me by train to home to Katowice at night.
The next morning, I go with you to Krakow.
I go with you to Krakow.
And there you will find your husband and all I can do.
And I didn't go.
I put her on the train in Katowice.
They went to Krakow.
And she found her husband.
He was in hiding already because they were looking for him, the Germans.
He was a very well-known Zionist.
They were looking for him already.
But they got away through Hungary, and they reached Palestine.
And they were safe.
The parents naturally probably went through the gas chambers or something.
We don't know.
And I decided I go to Berlin.
There must be somebody in Berlin who can give me papers so I can get away.
I went the first time to Berlin in spite it was strictly prohibited for a Jew to travel.
I got on the train, dressed up, and went.
I come to Berlin the first time.
Where do you go?
The Jewish Federation.
It was a small place there because most of the Jews were gone already in Germany.
What do you want?
I said, I'd like to talk to somebody from the Zionist organization.
There's no Zionist organization anymore.
But today is a delegate from Geneva, from Switzerland here.
And maybe you could talk to him.
I knock on the door.
An older man is sitting.
And he asked me what I want.
I explained to him who I am.
I am a Zionist and very well-known, active Zionist.
And I want to get out.
He said, you know, I can get you the papers.
But I wish you go back and you inform me what the Germans have intended to do with the Jews in Poland.
I was a good soldier.
I went back to Katowice.
Two days after, the Germans registered all Jewish men from 18 to 45.
And they told us on a certain day, we should be on the train.
And we would go to work for them.
The day before the transport was supposed to go, I said to my friend, I'm not going.
We got dressed up, and we got on the train again.
We went on the train all through Southern Germany to Austria.
And on the Austrian-Yugoslavian border, we got out of the train.
They were full of German military.
But they never stopped us, because we were well-dressed.
So when I had already a map from Vienna.
And Vienna prepared where we have to go in the mountains.
And right by the station was a little road right up in the mountains.
And we went up, climbed the mountains all night.
And early in the morning, it was pretty dark yet.
All of a sudden, a soldier, a Yugoslavian soldier.
And the Yugoslavian language is close to Polish, to  Czechoslovakian, to Russian, because it's a Slavic language.
So we communicated.
And we told them we are Polish officers.
He took us down to the border station in Yugoslavia.
And there the officers asked us questions-- who we are.
We said, Polish officers.
What do we want to do?
We want to join the Polish legion in France.
He said, fine.
But in order to go there, you need a visa.
In Zagreb, in the capital of Croatia-- this belonged to Yugoslavia-- it's still a Polish consul.
You go there, and you get your papers.
And you can go.
In order to go there, they gave us a soldier of the Yugoslavian Army who was supposed to take us from the border to the station.
And we could see the station maybe half a mile away.
We could see the station.
And he took us instead to the station.
He took us a few hundred feet maybe to the German border.
He handed us out to the Germans.
He was a Croat, a German spy.
See?
And this was the end of my liberty.
We were arrested, spent a couple days on the border.
And then they took us to Graz-- to the largest city in Southern Austria.
And in about 10 days, we were-- 10 days--
